web.title: How to Add a Donation Button to Your GitHub Repository - Donatr.ee
web.description: Step-by-step guide to adding donation buttons to GitHub repos. FUNDING.yml setup, README badges, GitHub Sponsors, crypto donations, and best ...
web.markdown:
You maintain an open-source project that thousands of people depend on. Maybe it is a library, a CLI tool, a framework, or a plugin. You spend nights and weekends fixing bugs, reviewing pull requests, and answering issues. And most of the time, you do it for free. That does not have to be the case. GitHub has built-in tools for accepting donations, and there are dozens of external platforms that integrate seamlessly with your repositories. This guide walks you through **every method to add donation and sponsorship buttons to your GitHub projects** in 2026. ## Method 1: GitHub Sponsors (the Built-in Option) GitHub Sponsors is GitHub's native funding platform. It adds a "Sponsor" heart button directly to your profile and repository pages, allowing supporters to set up monthly or one-time contributions without leaving GitHub. ### How GitHub Sponsors works - **Zero fees.** GitHub covers all payment processing costs. 100% of the sponsorship goes to you. - **Monthly or one-time.** You set sponsorship tiers (e.g., $5/month, $10/month) and supporters choose a tier or enter a custom amount. - **Integrated with GitHub.** The sponsor badge appears on profiles, and you can create sponsor-only repositories or content. - **Payouts via Stripe.** You connect a Stripe account to receive payments. Payouts go to your bank. ### How to apply GitHub Sponsors requires an application and approval process. Here is what you need: 1. Go to **github.com/sponsors** and click "Get started." 2. Fill in your profile: a short bio, what you work on, and why people should sponsor you. 3. Set up a Stripe Connect account for payouts. 4. Define your sponsorship tiers and any perks for each tier. 5. Submit and wait for approval (usually 1-7 days). **Important limitation:** GitHub Sponsors is not available in all countries. As of 2026, it supports roughly 70 countries, but many developers in Asia, Africa, and parts of Eastern Europe are excluded. If you cannot get approved, see our guide on [GitHub Sponsors alternatives](https://donatr.ee/blog/github-sponsors-alternatives) for other options. ## Method 2: The FUNDING.yml File The `FUNDING.yml` file is the most important mechanism GitHub provides for linking to donation platforms. When you add this file to your repository, GitHub automatically displays a "Sponsor" button at the top of your repo page with links to all the funding platforms you list. ### How to create FUNDING.yml 1. In your repository, create a directory called `.github` if it does not already exist. 2. Inside that directory, create a file named `FUNDING.yml` (the name is case-sensitive). 3. Add the platforms and usernames you want to display. ### Supported platforms in FUNDING.yml Here is the complete list of platforms GitHub natively supports in FUNDING.yml, along with the syntax for each: ``` # .github/FUNDING.yml # GitHub Sponsors username github: [your-github-username] # Patreon username patreon: your-patreon-username # Open Collective username open_collective: your-project-name # Ko-fi username ko_fi: your-kofi-username # Tidelift package coordinates tidelift: npm/your-package-name # Community Bridge project (Linux Foundation) community_bridge: your-project-name # Liberapay username liberapay: your-username # IssueHunt username issuehunt: your-username # Polar username polar: your-username # Buy Me a Coffee username buy_me_a_coffee: your-username # Thanks.dev username thanks_dev: gh/your-username # Custom URLs (up to 4) custom: [\ "https://donatr.ee/your-username",\ "https://your-site.com/donate",\ "https://paypal.me/your-username"\ ] ``` You do not need to fill in every field. Only include the platforms you actually use. Here is a realistic example for a developer who uses Ko-fi, has GitHub Sponsors, and wants to include their donatr.ee page: ``` # .github/FUNDING.yml github: [octodev] ko_fi: octodev custom: ["https://donatr.ee/octodev"] ``` ### What the Sponsor button looks like Once you push the FUNDING.yml file, GitHub parses it and adds a heart-shaped "Sponsor" button to the top of your repository page. When visitors click it, they see a dropdown with all the funding links you configured. Each platform shows its icon and name, making it easy for supporters to pick their preferred method. ### Organization-level FUNDING.yml If you maintain multiple repositories under a GitHub organization, you can create a default FUNDING.yml in the `.github` repository of your organization. This applies the funding configuration to all repositories in the organization that do not have their own FUNDING.yml. Individual repos can still override the default with their own file. ## Method 3: Donation Badges in README.md The FUNDING.yml button is visible on the repository page, but many people discover your project through your README file directly, whether on GitHub, npm, or documentation sites that mirror your README. Adding donation badges ensures your funding links are visible everywhere your README is displayed. ### Popular badge services Shields.io is the standard for generating badges. Here are the most common donation badges you can add: ``` ``` ### Where to place badges in your README The most effective placement is at the top of the README, right below the project title and description. Many popular projects place badges in a horizontal row like this: ```

``` This places your donation badge alongside your build status and version badges, which is where developers naturally look. ## Method 4: Crypto Donations in Your README Cryptocurrency donations are increasingly popular in the open-source community. Many developers prefer crypto because it is borderless, has no platform approval process, and avoids the country restrictions that GitHub Sponsors and similar platforms impose. ### Adding crypto addresses to your README The simplest approach is to add a "Support" section at the bottom of your README with your wallet addresses: ```

Support This Project

If this project saves you time, consider supporting its development:

Bitcoin (BTC) bc1qxy2kgdygjrsqtzq2n0yrf2493p83kkfjhx0wlh
Ethereum (ETH) 0x71C7656EC7ab88b098defB751B7401B5f6d8976F
Solana (SOL) 7EcDhSYGxXyscszYEp35KHN8vvw3svAuLKTzXwCFLtV
``` The drawback of listing addresses directly is that it clutters your README, and if you support multiple chains, the list gets long. A better approach is to link to a single page that displays all your crypto wallets and traditional payment methods together. ## Method 5: Using donatr.ee as Your Single Donation Link If you accept donations through multiple platforms and crypto wallets, managing separate links for each one becomes messy. Your FUNDING.yml can only hold 4 custom URLs, your README badges take up more and more space, and supporters have to guess which method you prefer. [Donatr.ee](https://donatr.ee/register) solves this by giving you one link — `donatr.ee/your-username` — that displays all your donation methods on a single page. PayPal, Ko-fi, Buy Me a Coffee, Patreon, crypto wallets (BTC, ETH, SOL, USDT), IBAN, Revolut, Wise, and more. Supporters pick whatever method works best for them. ### Setting up donatr.ee for your GitHub project 1. [Create a free donatr.ee account](https://donatr.ee/register) (takes under 2 minutes). 2. Add all your payment methods: crypto wallets, PayPal, Ko-fi, Patreon, IBAN, etc. 3. Optionally set a donation goal with a progress bar (e.g., "Server costs for 2026: $500"). 4. Add your donatr.ee link to FUNDING.yml, your README badges, and your profile. ### Adding donatr.ee to FUNDING.yml ``` # .github/FUNDING.yml github: [your-username] custom: ["https://donatr.ee/your-username"] ``` ### Adding a donatr.ee badge to README ``` ``` This approach is cleaner than listing 5-10 separate badges. One badge, one link, every payment method. Supporters see a professional donation page with QR codes, click-to-copy addresses, and clear instructions for each method. Check our [platforms page](https://donatr.ee/platforms) for the full list of supported payment methods. ## Method 6: Other Funding Platforms for Open Source Beyond GitHub Sponsors, several platforms are specifically designed for open-source funding. Each has different strengths depending on your project type and community. ### Open Collective Open Collective is built for transparent, community-funded projects. All transactions are public, and the platform handles fiscal hosting so informal groups can accept funds without creating a legal entity. It is popular with JavaScript libraries, developer tools, and community projects. Fees are 5-10% depending on the fiscal host. ### Liberapay Liberapay is a EU-based, open-source platform for recurring donations. It charges no platform fees (only payment processor fees of about 2-3%). It supports weekly, monthly, and yearly donations. Liberapay is popular in the European open-source community and is a strong option if you cannot access GitHub Sponsors. ### Ko-fi Ko-fi lets supporters send one-time donations or set up monthly memberships. The free tier takes 0% commission on one-time donations (PayPal/Stripe fees still apply). Ko-fi is quick to set up and does not require any approval process, making it ideal for developers who want to start accepting donations immediately. ### Polar.sh Polar is a newer platform built specifically for developers. It lets you monetize your open-source work through subscriptions, one-time payments, and issue funding. Supporters can fund specific issues in your repo, and you get paid when you close them. It integrates directly with GitHub issues and is worth watching as it matures. ### thanks.dev Thanks.dev takes a unique approach: sponsors set a monthly budget, and the platform automatically distributes it across all the open-source packages in their dependency tree. If your package is a dependency of a funded project, you receive a share without the sponsor needing to find and fund you individually. For a detailed comparison of all these platforms, read our guide on [GitHub Sponsors alternatives for developers](https://donatr.ee/blog/github-sponsors-alternatives). ## Best Practices for Donation Links in Open-Source Projects Adding donation links is only the first step. Where you place them and how you frame them makes a significant difference in whether people actually contribute. ### 1\. Put donation links in multiple places Do not rely on a single location. Add your funding links to: - **FUNDING.yml** — for the GitHub repo page Sponsor button. - **README.md** — both as a badge at the top and in a "Support" section near the bottom. - **CONTRIBUTING.md** — mention that financial support is also a way to contribute. - **Documentation site** — if you have a docs site, add a "Support" link in the navigation. - **Release notes** — when you ship a significant release, remind users that they can support the project. - **npm/PyPI/crates.io page** — most package registries display your README, so badges there are visible too. ### 2\. Explain what donations fund People are more likely to donate when they know exactly where the money goes. Instead of a generic "Support this project," try something specific: - "Donations cover server hosting ($40/month) and domain registration." - "Your support lets me dedicate 10 hours/week to maintaining this library." - "Goal: fund a security audit for v3.0 ($2,000)." Donatr.ee's progress bars are particularly effective here. When supporters see "Server costs: $320 / $500" with a visual bar, they understand the need and feel their contribution makes a tangible difference. ### 3\. Make it easy, not pushy Open-source users are sensitive to aggressive monetization. Keep your donation asks simple and respectful. A single badge in the README and a brief section at the bottom is plenty. Avoid popups, banners across your documentation, or guilt-tripping language. ### 4\. Offer multiple payment methods Your contributors are global. A developer in Germany might prefer IBAN. Someone in Japan might want to use crypto. A US-based developer might default to PayPal or GitHub Sponsors. The more methods you offer, the fewer people you exclude. This is exactly why a single aggregated link (like donatr.ee) outperforms listing individual platforms. ### 5\. Thank your sponsors publicly Recognition encourages more donations. Many projects add a "Sponsors" section to their README with logos or names of top supporters. GitHub Sponsors and Open Collective make this easy with widgets you can embed. Even a simple list of names goes a long way. ## Step-by-Step: Setting Up Everything from Scratch Here is the complete workflow to go from zero to a fully funded open-source project: 1. **Apply for GitHub Sponsors** if your country is supported. This gets you the native Sponsor button on your profile. 2. **Create a donatr.ee page** at [donatr.ee/register](https://donatr.ee/register). Add all your payment methods: PayPal, Ko-fi, crypto wallets, IBAN, and anything else you accept. 3. **Set up a donation goal** on donatr.ee with a progress bar that explains what the funds cover. 4. **Create .github/FUNDING.yml** in your repository with your GitHub Sponsors username and your donatr.ee link as a custom URL. 5. **Add a donation badge** to the top of your README pointing to your donatr.ee page. 6. **Add a "Support" section** at the bottom of your README with a brief explanation of what donations fund. 7. **Mention donations in CONTRIBUTING.md** so potential contributors know financial support is valued too. 8. **Push the changes** and verify the Sponsor button appears on your repo page. The entire setup takes about 15 minutes. After that, every visitor to your repository sees multiple, non-intrusive ways to support your work. ## Common Mistakes to Avoid - **Hiding donation links only in FUNDING.yml.** Many visitors never click the Sponsor button. Your README reaches a much wider audience, especially when rendered on package registries. - **Using only one payment method.** If you only offer GitHub Sponsors and a developer in Indonesia cannot access it, you lost a potential supporter. Always offer alternatives. - **Long crypto addresses without click-to-copy.** Pasting a 42-character Ethereum address as plain text in a README is error-prone. Use donatr.ee to provide click-to-copy addresses and QR codes instead. - **Forgetting to update links.** If you close a Ko-fi account or change a crypto wallet, update all the places you linked to it. Using a single donatr.ee link reduces this problem since you only need to update one page. - **Being apologetic.** You maintain software that people depend on. Asking for support is reasonable, not something to apologize for. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### Q: Does FUNDING.yml work for GitHub organizations? Yes. You can add FUNDING.yml to any repository, whether it belongs to a personal account or an organization. For organizations, you can also create a default FUNDING.yml in the `.github` repository that applies to all repos without their own file. ### Q: Can I accept crypto donations on GitHub without a third-party platform? There is no native crypto support in GitHub. You can display wallet addresses in your README, but for a proper experience with QR codes, click-to-copy, and multiple chains, a tool like [donatr.ee](https://donatr.ee/register) is much more effective. It gives you a single link that supports BTC, ETH, SOL, USDT, and other tokens alongside traditional payment methods. ### Q: How many custom URLs can FUNDING.yml hold? GitHub allows up to 4 custom URLs in the FUNDING.yml file. If you need more than 4 external links, use one of those slots for a donatr.ee page that aggregates all your methods in one place. ### Q: Is GitHub Sponsors available in my country? GitHub Sponsors availability varies by country and depends on Stripe availability in your region. As of 2026, it covers roughly 70 countries. If you are excluded, platforms like Ko-fi, Liberapay, Open Collective, and donatr.ee have broader international coverage. See our full comparison in [GitHub Sponsors alternatives](https://donatr.ee/blog/github-sponsors-alternatives). ## Start Getting Funded Today Open-source sustainability is not just about code. It is about making it as easy as possible for the people who benefit from your work to give back. With FUNDING.yml, README badges, and a tool like donatr.ee to aggregate everything, you can set up a complete funding infrastructure in under 15 minutes. **Ready to fund your open-source work?** [Create your free donatr.ee page](https://donatr.ee/register) and add every donation method your supporters might use: PayPal, Ko-fi, crypto wallets, IBAN, Revolut, and 15+ more. Set a donation goal with a progress bar, generate QR codes, and share one link everywhere. web.title: Displaying a sponsor button in your repository - GitHub Docs
web.description: You can add a sponsor button in your repository to increase the visibility of funding options for your open source project.
web.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/displaying-a-sponsor-button-in-your-repository#main-content)

# Displaying a sponsor button in your repository

You can add a sponsor button in your repository to increase the visibility of funding options for your open source project.

Copy as Markdown

## In this article

## [About FUNDING files](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/displaying-a-sponsor-button-in-your-repository\#about-funding-files)

You can configure your sponsor button by editing a `FUNDING.yml` file in your repository's `.github` folder, on the default branch. You can configure the button to include sponsored developers in GitHub Sponsors, external funding platforms, or a custom funding URL. For more information about GitHub Sponsors, see [About GitHub Sponsors](https://docs.github.com/en/sponsors/getting-started-with-github-sponsors/about-github-sponsors).

You can add one username, package name, or project name per external funding platform and up to four custom URLs. You can add one organization and up to four sponsored developers in GitHub Sponsors. Add each platform on a new line, using the following syntax.

| Platform | Syntax |
| --- | --- |
| [LFX Mentorship (formerly CommunityBridge)](https://lfx.linuxfoundation.org/tools/mentorship) | `community_bridge: PROJECT-NAME` |
| [GitHub Sponsors](https://github.com/sponsors) | `github: USERNAME` or `github: [USERNAME, USERNAME, USERNAME, USERNAME]` |
| [IssueHunt](https://issuehunt.io/) | `issuehunt: USERNAME` |
| [Ko-fi](https://ko-fi.com/) | `ko_fi: USERNAME` |
| [Liberapay](https://en.liberapay.com/) | `liberapay: USERNAME` |
| [Open Collective](https://opencollective.com/) | `open_collective: USERNAME` |
| [Patreon](https://www.patreon.com/) | `patreon: USERNAME` |
| [Tidelift](https://tidelift.com/) | `tidelift: PLATFORM-NAME/PACKAGE-NAME` |
| [Polar](https://www.polar.sh/) | `polar: USERNAME` |
| [Buy Me a Coffee](https://www.buymeacoffee.com/) | `buy_me_a_coffee: USERNAME` |
| [thanks.dev](https://thanks.dev/) | `thanks_dev: u/gh/USERNAME` |
| Custom URL | `custom: LINK1` or `custom: [LINK1, LINK2, LINK3, LINK4]` |

For Tidelift, use the `platform-name/package-name` syntax with the following platform names.

| Language | Platform name |
| --- | --- |
| JavaScript | `npm` |
| Python | `pypi` |
| Ruby | `rubygems` |
| Java | `maven` |
| PHP | `packagist` |
| C# | `nuget` |

Here's an example `FUNDING.yml` file:

```yaml
github: [octocat, surftocat]
patreon: octocat
tidelift: npm/octo-package
custom: ["https://www.paypal.me/octocat", octocat.com]
```

Note

If a custom URL in an array includes `:`, you must wrap the URL in quotes. For example, `"https://www.paypal.me/octocat"`.

You can create a default sponsor button for your organization or personal account. For more information, see [Creating a default community health file](https://docs.github.com/en/communities/setting-up-your-project-for-healthy-contributions/creating-a-default-community-health-file).

Note

Funding links provide a way for open source projects to receive direct financial support from their community. We don’t support the use of funding links for other purposes, such as for advertising, or supporting political, community, or charity groups. If you have questions about whether your intended use is supported, please visit [GitHub Support](https://support.github.com/).

## [Displaying a sponsor button in your repository](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/displaying-a-sponsor-button-in-your-repository\#displaying-a-sponsor-button-in-your-repository)

Anyone with admin permissions can enable a sponsor button in a repository.

1. On GitHub, navigate to the main page of the repository.

2. Under your repository name, click **Settings**. If you cannot see the "Settings" tab, select the  dropdown menu, then click **Settings**.



![Screenshot of a repository header showing the tabs. The "Settings" tab is highlighted by a dark orange outline.](https://docs.github.com/assets/cb-28260/images/help/repository/repo-actions-settings.png)

3. On the "General" settings page, in the "Features" section, select **Sponsorships**.

4. In the "Sponsorships" box, click **Set up sponsor button** or **Override funding links**.

5. In the file editor, follow the instructions in the `FUNDING.yml` file to add links to your funding locations.

6. Click **Commit changes...**

7. In the "Commit message" field, type a short, meaningful commit message that describes the change you made to the file. You can attribute the commit to more than one author in the commit message. For more information, see [Creating a commit with multiple authors](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/committing-changes-to-your-project/creating-and-editing-commits/creating-a-commit-with-multiple-authors).

8. Below the commit message fields, decide whether to add your commit to the current branch or to a new branch. If your current branch is the default branch, you should choose to create a new branch for your commit and then create a pull request. For more information, see [Creating a pull request](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/proposing-changes-to-your-work-with-pull-requests/creating-a-pull-request).



![Screenshot of a GitHub pull request showing a radio button to commit directly to the main branch or to create a new branch. New branch is selected.](https://docs.github.com/assets/cb-27122/images/help/repository/choose-commit-branch.png)

9. Click **Commit changes** or **Propose changes**.


## [Further reading](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/managing-your-repositorys-settings-and-features/customizing-your-repository/displaying-a-sponsor-button-in-your-repository\#further-reading)

- [About GitHub Sponsors for open source contributors](https://docs.github.com/en/sponsors/receiving-sponsorships-through-github-sponsors/about-github-sponsors-for-open-source-contributors)
- [FAQ with the GitHub Sponsors team](https://github.blog/2019-06-12-faq-with-the-github-sponsors-team/) on the GitHub Blog

![Ko-fi with Github.png](https://help.ko-fi.com/hc/article_attachments/23818023295901)

Have you thought about giving your GitHub visitors a way to support your work? Start your free Ko-fi page to earn an income and connect with your GitHub community.

![how-to-make-Ko-fi-your-Sponsor_2.gif](https://help.ko-fi.com/hc/article_attachments/18536503729821)

## Make Ko-fi your "Sponsor" funding link in your repository

GitHub lets you add your Ko-fi page as a funding link so visitors to your repository can support your work! Here’s how:

1. On **[GitHub.com](http://github.com/)**, head over to the main page of your repository.
2. Under your repository name, click **Settings**.
3. On the "General" settings page, in the "Features" section, select **Sponsorships**.
4. In the "Sponsorships" box, click **Set up sponsor button** or **Override funding links**.
5. In the file editor, follow the instructions in the `FUNDING.yml` file to add your Ko-fi page in the following format.![Screenshot 2024-04-30 at 09.26.41.png](https://help.ko-fi.com/hc/article_attachments/18536473060765)
6. Click **Commit changes** or **Propose changes**.

That’s it, anyone seeing your repository will be invited to support your project on your Ko-fi page.

![Github---Carousel-of-Ko-fi-bio1.gif](https://help.ko-fi.com/hc/article_attachments/18536503752605)

**[SacchanVRC](https://ko-fi.com/sacchanvrc) [Lotte](https://ko-fi.com/lotte) [The Narcolept](https://ko-fi.com/slugbot)**

## Signpost your Ko-fi page

Make it easy for people to find out how to support your work.

- **Add your Ko-fi link to your README:** Your project's README file is the first thing people see. Embed your Ko-fi link prominently, perhaps with a badge or a simple call to action like "Support this project on Ko-fi!"
- **Include Ko-fi in your repository descriptions:** A quick mention of Ko-fi support in your repo descriptions can direct potential supporters to take a look at your Ko-fi.
- **Mention Ko-fi in your GitHub Pages:** If you use GitHub Pages to showcase your projects, include a Ko-fi tip button or link on your site.

## How developers earn more on Ko-fi

Thousands of developers are using Ko-fi to receive one off and monthly tips to help support their projects. There are loads of other ways to make a meaningful income from your work.

- **Acknowledge supporters in your project:** Consider adding a Supporters section in your README or documentation, listing everyone who supports your project through Ko-fi.
- **Give early access to supporters:** Let your Ko-fi supporters get early access to new features or versions of your software.
- **Create a membership:** Connect with your most generous fans through **[Memberships](https://ko-fi.com/memberships)**. Give supporters perks like Discord roles, shout outs, steering group membership, early access etc.
- **Sell exclusive items or merch:** Open your **[Ko-fi Shop](https://ko-fi.com/shop)** to sell add-ons or merch to support your project.
- **Offer your services as requests:** If you’re a developer, you have in-demand skills. Open up [**Ko-fi Commission**](https://ko-fi.com/commissions) requests to let people request a code review or request your help with a custom project.

## Connect with your community

Talk with your supporters about your progress, thank them and get their input to improve your projects.

- **Update your community on your Ko-fi goals:** Use the GitHub Discussions feature to talk about your Ko-fi goals and celebrate milestones.
- **Celebrate achievements and thank supporters:** Whenever you hit a major milestone with your project, share an update thanking your Ko-fi supporters for helping to make it possible.
- **Engage with your supporters through Issues and Pull Requests:** Acknowledge those who contribute, not just with code, but also those who support your work through Ko-fi. A thank-you can go a long way.

With Ko-fi, every commit, every line of code, and every project update can become an opportunity to build a community of supporters and drive your projects forward. Let’s code, connect, and create together!

# [Donations & Sponsorships - Open Source Maintainership](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hILUhL_l_bU)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Dan on Open Source Software](https://www.youtube.com/@danonoss)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-06-02
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 27:47
**Views**: 389
**Likes**: 12
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
In this video I go through my advice and experience when it comes to handling donations & sponsorships while maintaining open source.
This isn't a full plan or a complete view of potential options, but some general advice followed by a tour through how I handle donations in my project which will hopefully provide some insight to other maintainers who are thinking about, or have started, going down the route of accepting donations and/or sponsorships.

Intro music: "Gnadenlosz Opening" by Manuel Senfft: https://music.tagirijus.de

► Elementary OS: https://elementary.io/
► KDE funding notification: https://www.gamingonlinux.com/2024/08/kde-plasma-62-adding-a-pop-up-for-donations-plus-they-want-to-make-a-next-generation-kde-os/
► KDE donation history: https://kde.org/community/donations/previousdonations/
► BookStack donations page: https://www.bookstackapp.com/donate/
► Gaining sponsors blogpost: https://danb.me/blog/gaining-sponsors/
► Find/Follow me: https://danb.me/links

00:00 Intro
00:47 High-level advice
04:02 Donation request examples
05:48 BookStack: History of donations
08:31 Current donation figures and trends
10:22 Displayed donation routes
15:43 GitHub sponsors
20:32 Ko-fi
23:07 2024 donations visualized
25:56 Blogpost, help & outro

#opensource #donations #software #funding #github #kofi
```

## Transcript

Hi, my name's Dan and today I thought
I'd do a video on donations. So, this
video is really targeted at open source
authors that are maybe thinking about
going down the route of donations or
maybe have even started trying to accept
donations and wondering what they could
maybe do better or what's going wrong.
And this isn't going to be a full guide
or anything. Uh, so first I'm going to
give some general highle advice just to
create some perspective about what I
think works well with donations and why
they might be difficult. And then after
that, I'm going to dig into my own
experience and journey of handling
donations and sponsorships within the
context of my own project, Book Stack.
And for the purpose of this video, I'm
going to essentially lump donations and
sponsorships into one bucket since they
are essentially the same thing. It's
people giving you money for very little
in return. I know with sponsorships,
maybe there's some like advertising
component, but there's a lot of overlap
in these two things. So, I'm just going
to lump them together. So, donations and
open source. Based upon what I see, I
think a lot of open source authors are
quite optimistic when it comes to
donations, at least in regard to the
high level theory. And this is probably
because a lot of developers in open
source are quite technical focused. They
focus on the code, the mechanics rather
than the social skills and the social
elements that may be needed with open
source maintainership and project
management in general. So I think it's
easy to have the notion that if you
build something great, then people will
contribute to that. they will donate
because of how awesome a project is,
because of how great it's built, because
of how wonderful the code is. And this
may be the case in some cases,
especially where it's a more technical
project where it's being used directly
by developers where you're giving them a

that we can currently see on the screen.
But but one of the main things that I
wanted to show this for is to encourage
people to not be shy in putting options
for those higher amounts. So don't worry
about putting up these values. you know,
when especially putting up that £500 a
month one, I thought that that's a
ridiculous amount of money that no one's
really going to go for. But then, of
course, people do go for it if it's an
option there, people might go for it,
and I'm very very grateful for that. In
fact, this sponsor here has been
sponsoring me for many years at this
point, at the highest tier, and they
even said that they don't want their
logo to show up. They're just happy to
support me. So, yeah, don't be afraid to
put on those high amounts there cuz
people may go for them. So, that covers
just about everything that I've got to
say about the sponsorships of my
projects. I do have a blog post here
about how I think I've gained a lot of
my larger sponsors. Cuz the reality is I
think it's quite random when it comes
down to exactly why people might have
donated to me. Some of them were
existing connections. Some of them were
companies where it may benefit to have
their logo on some of those higher
sponsor tiers. But ultimately, whatever
has their decision to provide that
sponsorship, you need to have that
community base there for your project.
and those sponsorship options to be
discovered. And sorry if I've drummed
that in a bit too much, but I think
that's just such an important element to
note that you do have to think about
that social side. It's not just a
technical endeavor. Otherwise, I hope
this provides some insight and some
thoughts to have in terms of how you're
thinking about doing your own
sponsorships or how you're managing your
existing sponsorships and donation
options. And if you have any questions,
feel free to ask them in the comments.
Um, I really do enjoy helping out other
open source maintainers cuz I know it
can be quite difficult and it can often
feel quite lonely as well in the world
of open source maintenance as a kind of
maintainer going by yourself, especially
as a lot of the content that I see is
from massive organizations like Red Hat
or Canonicle or the Linux Foundations,
you know, where they have a full
community meetup groups and things like
that where they can discuss a lot of
these things. So, if you're out there
just trying to craft it by yourself,
yeah, just feel free to give me a shout
and I'll try and help you, at least with
any insight that I can provide based on
my experience. But you can find me in
other places by going to my website,
danb.me. And from there, you can find
where I'm at on Masteron. And I've also
got my LinkedIn there, but I probably
won't respond on LinkedIn cuz I never
really visit it. But that's everything
for this video. So, hopefully this has
provided some insight and this has
helped you out. But other than that,
have a wonderful day.
[Music]
